rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT ET AL VS LAIDLAW TRANSIT INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant First Student, Inc.S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Evidence Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday June 10, 2015, Line 1, Defendant First Student, Inc.'s Motion For Evidence Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Tom Cohen, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is granted in part and denied in part as follows: Plaintiffs are precluded from introducing testimony at trial from any witness, whether a FSI employee or otherwise, where such witness or such testimony has not been identified in the form of a properly redacted memoranda or on lists produced by Plaintiffs to date. Put another way, Plaintiffs contend that they have fully complied with the Court's discovery orders. To the extent they have done so, such evidence may be used at trial regardless of when it was produced. To the extent they have not done so, any evidence improperly withheld as of this date, may not be used at trial. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tom@thomascohen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: PACIFIC BELL TELEPHONE COMPANY, A CALIFORNIA CORP et al VS. CITY OF SAN FRANCISCO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus Or, In The Alternative, Writ Of Mandate
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, June 10, 2015, Line PETITIONER PACIFIC BELL TELEPHONE COMPANY, A CALIFORNIA CORP's Motion For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus Or, In The Alternative, Writ Of Mandate The petition for writ of administrative mandamus is granted and denied in part. The court grants the writ as to the 33 permit applications that were denied because alternative sites existed. "It is well settled an administrative agency is bound by its own rules and regulations." Talmo v. Civil Service Com. (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 210, 218. The SMF Order is such a regulation. It is titled "Regulations for Issuing Excavation Permits for the installation of Surface-Mounted Facilities in the Public Rights-Of-Way." Petitioner correctly explains how the hearing process is supposed to work. (AR 2134). Petitioner had no obligation to propose multiple sites at the outset of the process. If other locations are proposed at the hearing, then the hearing officer has the discretion to keep the hearing open for an additional 20 days and entertain supplemental evidence. The hearing officers did not take advantage of this provision, however. The officers merely denied the applications and stated that other less intrusive alternatives were available. Respondent erred. When the petitioner's application complied with the other sections of the SMF Order (as they did in this case), the hearing officer and director had the authority to either approve the site or approve the site with changes. The MOU did not change this process. Respondent enacted Article 27 to insure that neighbors near the alternative sites would receive notice. The court denies the petition as to the 6 permit applications that were denied based upon the failure to comply with Article 27. Pursuant to Gov't 65922(b), the Permit Streamlining Act does not apply to administrative appeals within a state or local agency or to a state or local agency. Therefore, the "prospectively only" language of Cal. Gov't Code sec. 65942 does not apply to this case. Respondent did not violate Cal. Public Utilities Code sec. 5885(c)(2) because that provision applies to a completed application for an "encroachment permit" and this case involves the pre-application process for excavation permits. Lastly, petitioner does not establish that it was singled out. Article 27 applies to all SMF applicants including PG&E and Comcast. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ehg

rulings.Case Title: MARIA L BRICKER VS. JOYCE MOVING AND STORAGE, INC A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, June 10, 2015, Line 9, DEFENDANT JOYCE MOVING AND STORAGE, INC'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendant's demurrer is sustained in part and overruled in part. The demurrer to the first, second, and third cause of actions for conversion, negligence, and breach of contract are overruled. A bailee/bailor relationship was created and the statute of limitations has not yet run. Where a bailee has lawfully acquired possession of a bailor's property, the statute of limitations does not begin to run until the bailee willfully fails to redeliver the bailor's property upon the latter's demand. Here, Defendant lawfully acquired Plaintiff's property and failed to return the property when Plaintiff requested the return in 2014. Thus, the statute of limitations began to run in 2014 and Plaintiff's claim is ripe. [See Bufano v. San Francisco (1965) 233 Cal.App.2d 61,70.] The fourth cause of action for violation of the Public Utilities Code is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. PUC section 5113 states: "[t]he transportation of used household goods and personal effects in any truck or trailer for compensation over any public highway in this state is a highly specialized type of truck transportation. This chapter is enacted for the limited purpose of providing necessary regulation for this specialized type of truck transportation only, and is not to be construed for any purpose as a precedent for the extension of such regulation to any other type of truck transportation not presently so restricted." Plaintiff has not alleged in the complaint that Defendant transported the household goods. Here, the gravamen of the harm is the storage of Plaintiff's property, therefore the Public Utilities Code does not apply. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/EG

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers To Interrogatories And For Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiff Erika Mccartney And Her Attorney, Robert B. Hancock In The Sum Of $1,295.00 For Costs And Attorney Fees [Ccp §§ 2023.010, 2023.030; And 2030.300 ]
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday June 10, 2015, Line 5, DEFENDANT MANKE LUMBER COMPANY, INC.'S Motion To Compel Answers To Interrogatories And For Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiff Erika Mccartney And Her Attorney, Robert B. Hancock In The Sum Of $1,295.00 For Costs And Attorney Fees [Ccp §§ 2023.010, 2023.030; And 2030.300 ]. Pro Tem Judge Tom Cohen, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Motion is granted as to Special Interrogatories 1,3,6,9,12,13 and 15. The Motion is denied as to Interrogatories 16 and 17. It is not clear whether the determination of privilege, and whether certain individuals are experts in this regard, is before the court on this motion. The motion is denied without prejudice as to that issue. No sanctions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tom@thomascohen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JOSEPH JEWELL VS. 23ANDME, INC., A CORPORATION
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, June 10, 2015, Line 11, DEFENDANT 23ANDME, INC., A CORPORATION's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Motion for summary judgment granted. "The primary substantive provision of the [FAA] is section 2." (Rosenthal v. Great Western Fin. Securities Corp. (1996) 14 Cal.4th 394, 405.) Section 2 makes agreements to arbitrate "valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract." (9 U.S.C. § 2.) The California Supreme Court has explicitly held that "[S]ection 2 of the FAA applies regardless of which law the arbitration agreement incorporates." (Broughton v. Cigna Healthplans of Cal. (1999) 21 Cal.4th 1066, 1078.) Thus, case law makes clear that Section 2 of the FAA applies even if the contract contains a general choice of law provision. (See also Moses H. Cone Memorial Hosp. v. Mercury Const. Corp. (1983) 460 U.S. 1, "The effect of [Section 2] is to create a body of federal substantive law of arbitrability, applicable to any arbitration agreement within the coverage of the Act;" Sovak v. Chugai Pharms. Co. (9th Cir. 2002) 280 F.3d 1266, 1270, "[A] general choice of law clause within an arbitration provision does not trump the presumption that the FAA supplies the rules for arbitration.") U.S. Supreme Court cases establish that the requirement that the parties agree to class arbitration is substantive law, rather than merely procedural, because it furthers the objectives of Section 2 of the FAA. (Stolt-Nielsen S.A. v. AnimalFeeds Int'l Corp. (2010) 559 US 662, 682; AT&T Mobility LLC v. Concepcion (2011) 131 S.Ct. 1740, 1746.) Thus, because the general choice of law provision in the contract at issue here is insufficient to displace the substantive portions of the FAA, the FAA, and not the CAA, governs whether class arbitration may be compelled. The use of the words "you," "your," and "either party" in the arbitration agreement establishes that the contract is bilateral and does not provide for class arbitration. (See Nielsen v. Legacy Partners Residential, Inc. (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1115, 1130.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/eg
